label: yes
cot_prefix: Okay, let's see. The question is asking why Americans don't take all their vacation days. The options are A, B, C, D. First, I need to look through the passage to find the reasons given. The passage starts by saying that more than half of Americans don't take all their vacation days even though they think they need more holidays. So they want more, but don't take the days they have. Samuel Nahmias mentions that it's related to the economic situation in the US, with high unemployment (9.6%). People with jobs are unwilling to take time off because they might be worried about job security. The article says that those with jobs are "unwilling to take time off" because of the jobless numbers. So this points towards fear of losing their jobs, which is option B. Another part says that workers who do take breaks check emails frequently, which might make them feel like they can't fully disconnect. Also, the majority of workers who didn't take time off mentioned too many responsibilities or stress at work. Some also said they didn't have time to plan a holiday. So stress and responsibilities are factors, but the main reason mentioned first is the economic fear due to high unemployment rates. Technology is also a factor because smartphones allow constant contact, making people feel they need to stay connected. But the question is asking the primary reason according to the passage. The article starts by pointing out the economic situation and unemployment as the main cause. Option A is "they devote themselves to the whole work"—this could be a workaholic tendency, but the passage says it's due to economic reasons. The term "workaholics" is mentioned, but the explanation follows the economic situation as the root cause. Option C mentions being asked to keep in touch with bosses, but the passage says that workers who take breaks check emails, not that they are required to. It's more about their own anxiety to stay connected, not a company requirement. Option D says they aren't in need of holidays, but the passage states the opposite: they think they need one more
